summary: System design walkthrough by Sudhan Shu (senior engineering manager at Atlassian, ex-Meta staff SWE, ex-Microsoft principal SWE). Question: design sponsored ads experience at Google.com. Presented as a mock interview demonstration covering: functional requirements (sponsored ads definition, ranking system), non-functional requirements (availability, consistency, latency SLOs, durability), back-of-the-envelope calculations, high-level architecture (API gateway, Kafka, DynamoDB, Lambda, Redis), trade-offs (SQL vs NoSQL, sync vs async, batching, caching with Redis sorted sets), data modeling, deep dives (ambiguous search handling, new seller starvation, parallel Kafka consumers, correctness via message offset), monitoring/testing/deployment breadth, and staff-level interview calibration guidance.
quality_flags: Solo presentation format — Sudhan plays both interviewer and candidate; Q&A pairs extracted from rhetorical question-answer structure

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: All right. So, we are going to discuss a new question: design sponsored ads experience at google.com. So, this is a very vague and ambiguous problem statement provided to you by your interviewer. And think of it that you are a product manager talking to a customer figuring out the requirements in order to design sponsored ads experience at google.com.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: So first we'll collect the functional requirements for this question. As a candidate my first question would be: hey, what are sponsored ads? So sponsored ads let retailers advertise their products on Google and when a user like you and me click on it, Google will earn referral fees, right? So as an example, if I search iPhone in my browser, you can see sponsored ads, you know, on the site. My next question will be, can a sponsored ad contain products from different sellers? Yes, it can. And can a sponsored ad contain several products from one seller? For example, there can be multiple iPhone products from Amazon.com. And yes, the answer is it can — it can have products from multiple vendors as well as it can have multiple products from a single vendor in the ad. Is there a ranking system involved that selects which retailers to show in the sponsored ads? Yes, there's a ranking system. Let us say it's simple. It's based on the clicks. If the user clicks on the ad, the score of that ad will increase by five points and eventually this will be a feedback loop cycle that will keep on running and as and when the ad is scoring more and more points it will have more probability of being shown in the sponsored ads for that product. How many sellers and products we're talking about? Let's say 100 million sellers and 1 billion products.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Now let's go over the non-functional requirements. We want a system which is highly available — at least four nines of availability — and what does availability mean here: that sellers submitting the products for sponsored ads, the system should be available to intake those products in our system and process them; users searching for a product, let's say I go to google.com and I search iPhone, and I should be getting the sponsored ads, right, so the system should be highly available. And we can have a stretch goal of five nines of availability. Eventual consistency — so when you explain these non-functional requirements you have to be very clear in terms of explaining what it means in this current context — right, so eventual consistency here means rendering the ads based on the score. When a user one searches maybe he'll get result ABC, another user gets BCD, and let's say user one again searches, he or she gets BCD. But let's say BCD are the eventual consistent ads results based on the updated scores. So that's what I mean by eventual consistency here. The latency and score processing for the ads after ingestion of the clicks — let's say the SLO is 3 minutes at P99. Latency in rendering ads when user searches the ad on the browser — P99 less than 200 milliseconds. Latency in new ad processing — let's say a seller or a retailer is submitting an ad product — we'll call it P99 as 1 minute. Durability — we don't want any data loss in our pipeline. When user clicks on an ad or a seller or retailer submits an ad product, we need to ingest that — we don't need to lose the data in our systems and in the pipeline. The system should be fault tolerant. It should be scalable. Scalable here means that when the traffic is increased, the load is increased, number of users, number of sellers, number of ads per seller — our system should be able to handle those cases. Now, if we get some time, we can define here are the things below the cut line like the system should be frugal or cost-friendly. We need to take care about the security in the system because we're dealing with, you know, user data, you know, seller data, etc. So, we need to make sure our system is secure, but we'll get to that if only the time permits.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Now, design doesn't come for free. Scalability doesn't come for free. We need to do some data calculations or back of the envelope calculations. Right? Here, your interviewer is expecting you to come up with some reasonable assumptions like how many daily active users, how many daily active advertisers you are thinking about, right? And you're building a scalable system. So they expect some good judgment from you on these numbers. So search TPS — you know, let's say the daily active users searching for a keyword like iPhone, let's say it's 10 million. Average search per user per day let's say is 10. So that means that 10 million daily active users multiplied by 10 divided by 10 to the power 5 — so usually one day has roughly 86,400 seconds but I have rounded it off to 10 to the power 5 which is 100,000 — so if you divide you know daily active users and number of searches happening per day per user the answer is 1,000 requests per second. However peak can be 100x of that because usually when we see during the daytime in the geography, whatever geography we are part of, the calls are more than outside the business hours. So 100x translates to 100k requests per second at the peak TPS. Now ad creation TPS — let's assume daily active advertisers are 100k and average new ads per day per user is 10, or per seller in this case. So the TPS translates to 100k advertisers multiplied by 10 ads per advertiser divided by 10 to the power 5 which translates to 10 requests per second and again peak let's assume 100x so it's 1,000 per second. Now click events TPS — for that let's again say, okay, daily active users we already called out 10 million and average clicks per user per day on the sponsored ads is 10 — and this translates in a similar way to 1,000 clicks per second and peak is 100k clicks per second. Storage — let's say we need to store all this data for 10 years for audits, compliance, etc. And we know that we have daily active advertisers as 100k and then 10 ads per advertiser per day and let's ballpark, when one ad product we are storing in a database or storage layer it's 500 bytes — we'll do the data modeling down the line but at this point in time we're just taking a ballpark estimate. And then 365 days in a year, for replication at least three copies, and for 10 years — okay we need to store the data — this translates to roughly 5.475 terabytes of data we are talking about. It's good to talk about system properties now that we have done the back of the envelope calculations. The system seems to be more IO intensive — network and storage IO. It doesn't seem to be a compute intensive system. We are not doing image processing or running heavy compute algorithms on top of our processor. So the system seems to be more IO intensive rather than compute intensive.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Let's talk about the design. The high level design — very very high level design which is a 32,000 ft overview. So let's call — this client is a user and this client is a seller or retailer. Okay. So we'll first go with this client, you know, seller or retailer submitting their new ads to our system. So our first layer will be an API gateway. The responsibilities of API gateway will be rate limiting to prevent us from denial of service attacks or distributed denial of service attacks, authentication, authorization and load balancing. So what happens is, you know, when they want to upload their ad product they need to provide a small image for that ad or a thumbnail — right — first it will get a pre-signed URL from the ad service and it will directly upload to the S3 object storage. We can use S3 in AWS or equivalent in Azure or GCP. So once it's uploaded it will send the whole payload — hey, what's the ad, what is the content of the ad, what is the product etc — to the ad service and after that we have an ad creation topic on Kafka, you know, streaming system and we have a consumer of that Kafka and it will put that an ad record in a database. So as and when the record goes in the database we have a lambda in AWS or Azure function or equivalent in GCP running — which is equivalent to for example triggers in relational database — here we call it like DDB streaming, you know, in DynamoDB case, or equivalent we can have a function which is a serverless function which will process the new entry and based upon that, you know, it will put the data in the cache so that the search is faster. We can read it from the cache for that product type — what ads we have — and before it adds to the database here we need to make sure for a new ad we add some by default some score for that ad and that is where, you know, after the score is added and database has all the elements we process it through a serverless lambda or Azure function and it goes to the cache. The cache will hold, you know, the top 20 for that product type for example, or top 100 or top 25 — some very minimal number, whatever you want to show in terms of sponsored ads, you know, based upon the score. And let's go with a flow, you know, where a user basically clicks on an ad — again through API gateway it will go to click service and the clicks will be, you know, handled by a Kafka topic and there will be a consumer which is a clicks processor which will consume these clicks and based on the clicks incoming, you know, it will have some rules cached in it which will have some TTL time and it will allocate — hey, this ad is clicked, let me give it, you know, plus 30, plus 50, plus 5, you know, whatever score scoring system we have — and based upon the rules defined in the scoring system. And based upon that, it will update, you know, your ad score in the database. Again, as and when score is updated, you know, it will call the serverless lambda and again cache will be refreshed. Let's say this ad has got a good score and it comes in top 25 or top 100 — then this cache will be updated just to hold the top 25 or top N entries for that product type. Now the last one is, you know, a client user searching a product in browser — then it will go to search service through API gateway, first it will look into the cache — hey, is that product type in the cache, let me get the results faster — maybe top 10, top 30 depending on the screen we are using whether it's a, you know, laptop, whether it's a mobile device or an app, you know, depending on that it can return the results right. And if this cache hit doesn't go through then we have a cache miss scenario which means that for that product type we'll just call the database and get the data from the database and render to the client. So this is a very 32,000 ft overview of the design, very high level design. We will go into the internals, we will understand the internals and we'll talk about various trade-offs. So let's jump into it.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Our first trade-off will be the choice of database — you know, whether we are going with a relational database or a non-relational database. Right? So in this case if you see we don't have very hard transactional requirements. We are just getting clicks and this click processor is updating the ad, you know, based upon the updated score. So it's not like, you know, several users in a concurrent fashion for example trying to update their bank account, or some system where concurrency and transactions are important. But in this case we just have click processor only as the only entity updating the ad score in the database. So we should be good, you know, with this model. So we don't want a strict transactional system here. Data velocity and volume is high by the way. So NoSQL databases like DynamoDB also support transactions. However, we don't need transactions in this case as I said. But any scale-out DB, you know, on the cloud like DynamoDB or equivalent in Azure or GCP will work in this case.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: What about database sharding? So when we did some back of the envelope calculations above we notice our data storage is around 5.5 terabytes. Usually we do sharding if the data storage is more than 50 terabytes. So based upon the data storage in terms of terabytes we don't need sharding. However the write requests — which means like clicks — number of clicks coming to our system at peak is 100k. Ad creation is at peak 1,000 per second. In total we have updates happening at around 100k per second at peak. This doesn't do well if we don't shard our database. So sharding is required generally when write requests are more than 10k, 15k or 20k per second. Hence in light of, you know, the number of write requests we're getting per second, we'll be sharding our database.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Next is synchronous versus asynchronous processing. So for the ad creation if you see we have a peak of 1,000 requests per second. Now based upon that you might not just use a Kafka — it's not required to have a pub-sub system, you know, sitting in — you can just process the request, you know, create an ad in database and process it synchronously, right, so maybe Kafka sitting in between can be an overkill. But however I thought, you know, once we receive a new ad there might be several validation steps that needs to be executed — if the ad is duplicate or not, trust and safety, compliance, etc — and hence I thought it might take more things to be done and we can process the ad asynchronously and customer experience will be better because we can just process it asynchronously and we can say hey we received your ad we are going to process it and latency that they see on their side will be significantly lower. But I mean there is no right and wrong answer in system design so you can still say I'll do synchronous processing given that number of requests is only 1,000 per second and as per requirements we are not given to handle the duplicate checks, trust and safety issues, etc. So, if you say I want to do synchronous processing because of this and that, that will also work well in this case. Now for clicks if you see we have a peak of 100,000 per second. Now async processing will be really good for processing the clicks because the meat of the problem is to let the user see the ads. Clicks we can process in an async way — you know, it can decouple the processing of the clicks and we can also batch the clicks before we update the database. By consuming the clicks in a way that hey we consume the clicks, we batch them, and then we make a one batch call to the database — and that way we can reduce the load on the database, we can do any number of things. So I feel it's good to do the clicks processing in async way and if our peak is even more than that, let's say 1 million per second, generally it would be better if we do an async processing.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Now batching versus non-batching of the clicks. So we know that our latency SLO for processing the clicks and updating the ad score is 3 minutes. We can leverage this SLO, right, in a way that you can batch the clicks for example based upon 30 seconds, or if the number of clicks we have got to process is 10,000 — all of this is configurable, right, maybe 15 seconds or 1 minute, you know, whatever occurs faster — based upon the time or number of clicks we have to process. And then we can just aggregate the scores, you know, across the ads. For example in 10,000 clicks we have 5,000 clicks for one ad — we can just create — okay if the click yields plus five for that ad and if there are 5,000 clicks for ad one we'll have 25,000 as a score to be added for ad one. And again, you know, this will reduce the load on the database and that's where it's good to do the batch API call — and batching will really help in this case.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: What about caching? So we have Redis or Memcached as our options. So I'll prefer Redis in this case because Redis has some good data structures like sorted sets which we want to use — we don't need to reinvent the wheel by implementing these in Memcached. It's a very plain or vanilla cache. So, sorted set is a combination of hash table and skip list. So hash table will have the entry which is the ad and the score for that ad and skip list will have, you know, the category — which means for example iPhone — and the sorted list of ads, you know, sorted by scores. And in terms of cache storage again we can do some back of the envelope calculation here. Let's assume we have 100,000 product types like stationary, comic book, iPhone etc. And we want to store top 25 per product type. And let's assume record size is 1 KB per ad entry. And which translates to 100k product types multiplied by 25 and then 1 KB record size — which is 2.5 GB — which we can easily put in cache. So nowadays we have systems which can have 1 terabyte of cache easily. Yes, cache is expensive, but, you know, we'll have faster results because it will be an in-memory processing and we can provide a better customer experience and reduce latency to our customers.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: So, let's do some data modeling here. In terms of data modeling, if you see it, we have an ads table. We have ad ID which is the primary or partition key. We have a product ID, product type can be iPhone, we have a title for that ad, price for that ad, image which is a thumbnail for that ad, seller name, seller ID, score for that ad, some metadata, creation time and update time. We need to create index here. The index here we are creating is on product type and score. So product type is the partition key for that index and the score is the sort key. These are very DynamoDB specific things but in general if you say I'm creating index on product type and score which will be good enough. Because if you do the onion peeling things work in a similar way. So why do we need this index? Because let's say we have a cache miss and the cache is not having — let's say if a product type iPhone — let's say cache restarted or something happened with the cache or it's a product type not found in the cache for now — then we have to fall back on the database. In order to make sure our database queries are faster with low latencies, we can just use this index. Hey, for this product type sorted by score, give me top 10, top 25, right? It's still better than searching the whole table and then finding out who are the top 25. So that way we are leveraging index for goodness. Now, if you see, I've selected ad ID as a primary key for the table, not the seller ID. I'm not storing all the seller ads or ad products in a single partition because I don't want hot partitions or non-uniform partitions, right? Because several sellers can have more ads or ad products than other sellers and this will cause non-uniform partitions. With ad ID being a primary key, you know, we are making sure that we are creating uniform partitions in this case.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: So now comes the interesting part of the question which is we'll do several deep dives. Our first is: how to handle the case when a user searches 'gifts for 10 year old'? And before we go into deep dives, you know, usually your interviewer expects you to identify some good areas around your design and they expect you to find the most interesting components of your design and then you at least deep dive into a few of them, right, showing the depth of your knowledge. And that is why we are focusing on deep dives now.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: So how to handle the case when user searches for example 'gifts for 10 years old'? You can see I've searched gifts for 10 years olds and I'm getting multiple sponsored products. So the first approach is — we can add a tags field to the database. For example, if it's a comic book product type, someone is creating an ad for that — the product type is comic book — we can add tags in our database like 'gifts for 10-year-old' or 'children's gift' etc. And when the ad entry is added to the database we know that this lambda will process and add to the cache based upon the score. And this will add in the Redis cache and there will be three sorted sets. For example for comic book I have these many ads, and for 'gifts for 10 year old' we are having again the same entries right, and 'children's gift' also all the three same entries. If someone searches 'gifts for 10 years old' I can just look into the cache, hey if there's an entry for this just get me that list and return it. Now this approach is good but it has several limits — right, for example you cannot predict what the user will be searching for. They can search for example 'gifts for maybe 11 years old' or 'give me a gift for my child'. Anything can be like that right? So you cannot predict what user can do in their query. So in light of this, you know, this approach is not very solid and also it is causing repeated data entries which can cause sync issues and redundant data is there and will lead to more memory consumption and duplication. So we have another approach — we can add a machine learning based context service. Whenever the user searches a keyword the context service will return some relevant product types, right. For example, if someone searches 'gifts for 10 years old', you know, this query will be passed to context service — it will return some keywords for example 'stationary', 'comic book', or 'a toy', etc. And based upon that the search service will just search in the cache for those product types, get top 10 for, you know, three product types for example 'comic book', 'stationary' and 'toy', and we'll sort the overall results in memory and we'll return the response to the user. So this way it's more extensible, it's better — you know, you don't have to rely on what user is going to search — and no data replication or more memory consumption etc.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: So our next deep dive is: how to handle the case when there are no results for the product — for example, 'pencil'. Now if I go to google.com and I search pencil, if you see there is no sponsored products returned. Right? So we'll keep it simple — just return no results as is.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: How to handle a case to ensure new seller ads are shown to the customer? You know, let's say I'm a new seller onboarding and I'm submitting a new sponsored ad product, right, for a product type which is quite popular and which people have already clicked. And if I'm a new entry into the market, you know, Google will not show my product because my score has not been generated — I don't have any score — and for this product type the sellers who have more score for their products will eventually be shown. So it's more like a starvation problem, right. So how do I deal with such a case? For any new seller ad, provide a default score which is more than the current maximum score for the product type so that we give them a fair chance. And this will ensure that new sellers or new ads are promoted and not being starved in our system. And that is what, you know, we want to have because as a business we want different sellers to get products in our system so that we can have sponsored ads and if they click on that, if users click on that, you know, we get — as Google as a business — we get referral fees.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: All right. So next is how to handle and process millions of incoming clicks per minute or maybe 30 seconds. So it's a million requests coming to us and if you think about the design, you know, when we get the clicks we're processing the click and just adding the score and updating the database. So we are making an IO call which is expensive and generally how a Kafka consumer works is, you know, it consumes a message, it processes it, then it consumes the next message, processes it — and it works in a sequential way, right. So if there are millions of clicks coming per minute we might have, you know, higher latency in processing them. So how do we make sure we process the clicks very fast also given that we are doing an IO call to process each and every click? Or even in the batch way we are processing a batch, then we update the database, and then we process the next batch — so it's all going in a sequential way how Kafka consumer works. So the solution here is to have a load balancing design. Let's say how Kafka works — Kafka has several broker machines and let's say we have 10 partitions for click topic in Kafka and these partitions are P0 to P9, all 10 partitions. We can create multiple instances of our service which is click processor — let's call it click processor 1, 2, 3, 4 and 5. This is just for demonstration purposes. It's extensible. You can create as many as you want. But think about like, you know, each cluster has one primary and two secondaries. And if primary goes down then secondary becomes primary. You can have a ZooKeeper like system which is monitoring this. But overall the idea is that we load balance how many partitions are being consumed by a primary right. So which means that if we do 2:1 — again for demonstration we can have 3:1, 1:1, right, it's extensible system we are trying to build — but for the example let's say we are doing 2:1 mapping which means two Kafka partitions to one click processor instance mapping. So P0, P1 are being consumed by click processor one, P2, P3 by two, P4, P5 by click processor 3, and then P6, P7 by click processor 4 and P8, P9 by click processor 5. That way we are doing parallel processing. We are moving fast and within a partition the messages are in order. So if our partition key is let's say based upon an ad product — this means that for all the clicks happening for that ad it is coming in in order and we are processing in order. We are not doing out of order processing within a partition. So that way you can scale your system and you can process, you know, the messages faster and you can enable parallel processing.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: So our next deep dive will be: how to ensure correctness in processing the ad score. Let's talk about the regular flow, you know, in the click processor — how it works. As I said it consumes the clicks, you know, till the batch size or the time window is elapsed — it does in-memory score updates for the different ads in that batch and then it will call the batch API of the database to update the scores of different ads and after it is done it will basically commit those messages in Kafka. You know, what is the maximum offset — so every message in Kafka has an offset — so if you commit that, all the messages behind that offset or message ID automatically gets removed from Kafka. It means that they have been processed and they can be deleted. So this is the regular flow — it consumes the batch, it updates the scores for different ads in the batch, and then after database is updated with the updated ad scores for different ads in the batch it will commit the offset in Kafka and Kafka will delete those messages and after some restart or failover happens we can just process from the ads which are not processed in general. So the situation is: let's say the click processor updates the ad scores in the database after processing the batch but it didn't commit the offset in Kafka — or the messages which it has processed, it forgot or it missed telling to Kafka that hey I processed these messages — and the primary click processor crashes. It can crash because of many reasons — hardware, software, power, networking, etc. But let's say it was not able to commit. Now upon restart, click processor will again process the uncommitted messages, you know, thinking that they're not being processed and the ad scores will be incorrectly updated. In this case, you know, the scores for the ads which have been processed, you know, it will lead to doubling the score. Not doubling the entire score, but doubling, you know, whatever number it had previously when it processed the batch — and it can cause accuracy, correctness issues, you know, bias in our system. So how do we handle such a case? So the solution here is to add, you know, a new field in the database called 'message offset' for every ad and before processing, you know, the click event per ad the click processor will check if the message offset of the ad click event is greater than the message offset for that ad in the database. Now if it is — the one which we are processing is greater than — then sure we can process it because it's a new message. But if it is less than or equal to what the offset we have stored in the database it will ignore that click event because it's already been processed. So let's go over an example to make things, you know, clearer. So let's say we have this Kafka topic and we have several messages coming with offset 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6 and initially we have ad ID 1, 1, then 2, 1, 3, and 1. So after initial processing of this batch, you know, this will be the state of our ads table. Ad ID one the score is 20 because we have four instances of ad one and plus five for each click event — so five multiplied by four is 20. Similarly ad ID 2 is offset three, just one instance, so score is five. Ad ID three also has score of five. The maximum offset for ad ID one we have six, ad ID two we have three, and ad ID three we have five. So this is how, you know, the database is updated. And let's say the click processor didn't commit the offset in Kafka and click processor failed over or restarted — then it will start to reprocess these messages. Now during the reprocessing of the messages it will ignore all the messages for ad ID 1 because, you know, offset one is less than six. Similarly offset 2, 4 and 6 are also less than or equal to six. We need a message or a click event which is at an offset more than the one which we have already processed for this ad. So using this technique, you know, we can make sure we have correctness in our system and we are ensuring that things are accurate. So we can have more deep dives as well — for example, how to handle huge write loads for a given product type. Let's say Apple launched a new iPhone and many sellers are trying to, you know, sell that iPhone or multiple users are clicking for that iPhone. How do we process huge write loads in our system? That is another deep dive we can consider but I'll leave it up to you to think about a solution around it.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Final design and recap. So this is our final design basically. So again let's do a recap — the seller client who is submitting the new ad, you know, it will go through API gateway which does the rate limiting, authentication, authorization, load balancing. Again we are processing it through Kafka — you can just remove Kafka if you want and give a reasoning — but as I said there's no right and wrong answer in the design, it's more about the justification you give. In this case I'm using Kafka because I think in processing there might be many things we have to do — for example compliance, trust and safety, there can be many business logics around it written in ads processor. So I don't want to hold the TCP connection for long. I want to process async for better customer experience and low latency. And once it's processed, I'm creating a new ad in DynamoDB. And I'm adding a new record for this ad. And this score will be the maximum score, current maximum score for that particular product type which can be got from the index. Cool. So next is, you know, the client who is the user who is going to browser and searching an ad product or clicking an ad product. Let's say they click on one of the sponsored ad products — it will go to click service. This click service will, based upon the ad ID as partitioning key, route the Kafka partitions — it will be routed to the right partitions based upon the hash algorithm that Kafka uses based upon the partitioning key, in this case which is the ad ID. To handle the huge load, you know, I've created several instances as we demonstrated earlier and the right primary, you know, will process the in-order messages for that partition and do the batching based upon the time limit or number of messages in the batch. For example if number of messages reached is 10,000 or 30 seconds have passed, whichever is faster — it will process that batch and aggregate the score per ad and make a one batch API call to update the record in DynamoDB and this reduces the load on our database. And before that it can refer to the scoring rule system — there is a — it can cache the scoring rules with some TTL. Now once the record is added here, you know, new entry, there will be a DynamoDB streaming process through lambda and lambda will update the Redis cache in this case and it will maintain a sorted set for each product type — what are the top, you know, 25 or top N based upon the score for that product type. And the user again searching on browser will go to search service — first it will resolve, you know, the keyword, from there it will, for example for 'gifts for 10 years old' or 'children's gift' it will give some relevant product types, and it will search the product types in cache. If it is able to find that then well and good. If it is not able to find that it will refer to DynamoDB. Let's say the cache crashes — it's in-memory data, there's no data, cache needs to build up. So in case of cache miss we will go to the index for this product type, we'll get based upon the score sorted scores, we'll get top N or top 25, top 50, whatever we want to get and we can just give it to the client right. And there might be some product types which are not commonly used or searched for — we don't want to put them in cache to save on cost, we can just go to DynamoDB. The only trade-off is that we'll be able to search — I mean slower than cache — but it will be still faster because of the index availability. One thing I missed is that before creating a new ad we need to get a pre-signed URL and first upload the image to S3 and then send the payload. The advantage here is that we are not sending the whole image payload to ad service and then it is updating to S3 — it's better that we just update it. And pre-signed URLs are pretty secure. It has some time limits for how long it's valid, what IP addresses can access it. So, it's a very safe and secure mechanism that AWS has provided. So, that's our design overall.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: We also want to cover some breadth. For example, monitoring. It's good to talk about some technical and business metrics that, you know, we should care about in this system design. Technical metrics like CPU utilization, memory or heap utilization, number of threads, number of pending messages in Kafka to see how we are processing the messages, end-to-end processing latency for ads creation, click event processing, you know, cache hit percentage for that search. Some business metrics like number of clicks per ad, search latency customer-facing, seller ad creation customer-facing latency, customer reported incidents, some qualitative feedback from the customer using the sponsored ad products, etc. Testing — you can do unit testing with more than 90% line and branch coverage, integration testing, performance testing, scale testing, alpha beta testing. For deployments, you know, you can dial up — you know, based upon the customers — assuming it's a brand new system, you can start with 1%, you know, get some feedback, observe the metrics, right? And then dial it up slowly to 5%, 10%, 25%, 50%, 70%, and 100% with proper soak period across the dial-ups. If time permits you can also talk about how you can be cost friendly or frugal and how to handle the security in the system. So these are some breadth topics which you can talk about, you know, with your interviewer.

turns.speaker: UNKNOWN
turns.text: With that, also I want to cover, you know, how staff plus people are calibrated for system design interviews. So you need to lead and drive the interview and yet be collaborative. A good thing here would be that in order to be collaborative, you can have some checkpoints — hey, do you have any questions? By the way, I'm going to take a pause if you want to ask any question, right? But don't take too many pauses. You know, you might want to take two or three pauses to make sure that you are leaning towards the right direction and you're covering the topics your interviewer cares about. The solution should be good and mature. A system design question can have several solutions, right? But for staff or staff plus roles, you need to make sure your solution which you are proposing is good, mature, extensible. Third is, you know, again depth is very important when it comes to staff or staff plus roles. You need to identify at least two to three interesting areas of the problem and dive deep into them. And the next part is — interviewer walks away with some new learnings or insights from you as a candidate. Time management is very important. You know there are many things you can be stuck on when you do the system design questions. So make sure you wisely use your time and time management is really critical. You need to showcase, you know, a good product mindset not just technical. I have seen many candidates who, you know, don't take product requirements seriously, don't demonstrate good product mindset and they don't go into a positive result. Basics should be really good. For example, you need to do ambiguity resolution. Ask good clarifying questions. Iron out the requirements. The problem statement being given to you is deliberately put vague. They want to see how you're going to clarify the requirements. Determine the scope, you know, for the problem in hand. Back of the envelope calculations. As I said, scalability doesn't come for free. You need to make sure, you know, you need to design your system accordingly. For example, if I want to store, you know, 50 terabytes of data, I'm not going to store it in memory, right? So, you need to do these calculations very nicely in order to, you know, do the design correctly because based upon these data or back of the envelope calculations, your design will change significantly. Component responsibilities — make at least share four to five trade-offs in your design to show off your thought process. You need to have a working solution. Demonstrate the breadth of your knowledge — you can talk about monitoring, deployments, testing, security, cost, etc. And lastly, you should be very good in collaboration and communication. Your interviewer is trying to see whether you have good values or not and if they're comfortable working with you in the team setting. So, that was it from today — where we designed sponsored ads experience at Google. Thank you for listening to it. I hope you all enjoyed this session. If you want to do a mock interview with a coach who's worked at your target company, just go to IGotAnOffer.com.

training_pairs.question: Design sponsored ads experience at google.com.
training_pairs.answer: So first we'll collect the functional requirements for this question. As a candidate my first question would be: hey, what are sponsored ads? So sponsored ads let retailers advertise their products on Google and when a user like you and me click on it, Google will earn referral fees, right? So as an example, if I search iPhone in my browser, you can see sponsored ads on the site. My next question will be, can a sponsored ad contain products from different sellers? Yes, it can. And can a sponsored ad contain several products from one seller? For example, there can be multiple iPhone products from Amazon.com. And yes, the answer is it can — it can have products from multiple vendors as well as it can have multiple products from a single vendor in the ad. Is there a ranking system involved that selects which retailers to show in the sponsored ads? Yes, there's a ranking system. Let us say it's simple. It's based on the clicks. If the user clicks on the ad, the score of that ad will increase by five points and eventually this will be a feedback loop cycle that will keep on running and as and when the ad is scoring more and more points it will have more probability of being shown in the sponsored ads for that product. How many sellers and products we're talking about? Let's say 100 million sellers and 1 billion products.

training_pairs.question: What are the non-functional requirements for this system?
training_pairs.answer: We want a system which is highly available — at least four nines of availability — and what does availability mean here: that sellers submitting the products for sponsored ads, the system should be available to intake those products in our system and process them; users searching for a product, let's say I go to google.com and I search iPhone, and I should be getting the sponsored ads, right, so the system should be highly available. And we can have a stretch goal of five nines of availability. Eventual consistency — so when you explain these non-functional requirements you have to be very clear in terms of explaining what it means in this current context — right, so eventual consistency here means rendering the ads based on the score. When a user one searches maybe he'll get result ABC, another user gets BCD, and let's say user one again searches, he or she gets BCD. But let's say BCD are the eventual consistent ads results based on the updated scores. The latency and score processing for the ads after ingestion of the clicks — let's say the SLO is 3 minutes at P99. Latency in rendering ads when user searches the ad on the browser — P99 less than 200 milliseconds. Latency in new ad processing — let's say a seller or a retailer is submitting an ad product — we'll call it P99 as 1 minute. Durability — we don't want any data loss in our pipeline. The system should be fault tolerant. It should be scalable. Scalable here means that when the traffic is increased, the load is increased, number of users, number of sellers, number of ads per seller — our system should be able to handle those cases. Below the cut line if time permits: the system should be cost-friendly, and we need to take care about the security in the system.
training_pairs.follow_up: Walk me through the back of the envelope calculations.

training_pairs.question: Walk me through the back of the envelope calculations.
training_pairs.answer: So search TPS — let's say the daily active users searching for a keyword like iPhone, let's say it's 10 million. Average search per user per day let's say is 10. So that means that 10 million daily active users multiplied by 10 divided by 10 to the power 5 — so usually one day has roughly 86,400 seconds but I have rounded it off to 10 to the power 5 which is 100,000 — so if you divide daily active users and number of searches happening per day per user the answer is 1,000 requests per second. However peak can be 100x of that. So 100x translates to 100k requests per second at the peak TPS. Now ad creation TPS — let's assume daily active advertisers are 100k and average new ads per day per seller is 10. So the TPS translates to 100k advertisers multiplied by 10 ads per advertiser divided by 10 to the power 5 which translates to 10 requests per second and again peak let's assume 100x so it's 1,000 per second. Now click events TPS — daily active users 10 million and average clicks per user per day on the sponsored ads is 10 — this translates to 1,000 clicks per second and peak is 100k clicks per second. Storage — let's say we need to store all this data for 10 years for audits, compliance, etc. 100k advertisers, 10 ads per advertiser per day, 500 bytes per ad, 365 days, three copies for replication, 10 years — this translates to roughly 5.475 terabytes of data. The system seems to be more IO intensive — network and storage IO — rather than compute intensive.
training_pairs.follow_up: Walk me through the high-level design.

training_pairs.question: Walk me through the high-level architecture.
training_pairs.answer: The high level design — very very high level design which is a 32,000 ft overview. So this client is a user and this client is a seller or retailer. Okay. So we'll first go with the seller or retailer submitting their new ads to our system. Our first layer will be an API gateway. The responsibilities of API gateway will be rate limiting to prevent us from denial of service attacks or distributed denial of service attacks, authentication, authorization and load balancing. So what happens is when they want to upload their ad product they need to provide a small image for that ad or a thumbnail — right — first it will get a pre-signed URL from the ad service and it will directly upload to the S3 object storage. So once it's uploaded it will send the whole payload — hey, what's the ad, what is the content of the ad, what is the product etc — to the ad service and after that we have an ad creation topic on Kafka and we have a consumer of that Kafka and it will put that an ad record in a database. So as and when the record goes in the database we have a lambda in AWS or Azure function or equivalent in GCP running — which is equivalent to for example triggers in relational database. Based upon that it will put the data in the cache so that the search is faster. Before it adds to the database we need to make sure for a new ad we add some by default some score for that ad. And then the cache will hold the top 25 or top N entries for that product type. For a user clicking on an ad — again through API gateway it will go to click service — the clicks will be handled by a Kafka topic and there will be a click processor which will consume these clicks, have some scoring rules cached with some TTL, and update the ad score in the database. Again, as and when score is updated, it will call the serverless lambda and cache will be refreshed. For a user searching a product in browser — they go to search service through API gateway, first look into the cache, if product type is in the cache get the results faster — top 10, top 30 depending on the screen. If cache miss, fall back to the database.
training_pairs.follow_up: What's your choice of database and why? And do we need sharding?

training_pairs.question: What database would you choose — relational or non-relational — and do we need sharding?
training_pairs.answer: In this case if you see we don't have very hard transactional requirements. We are just getting clicks and this click processor is updating the ad based upon the updated score. So it's not like several users in a concurrent fashion trying to update their bank account or some system where concurrency and transactions are important. In this case we just have click processor only as the only entity updating the ad score in the database. So we don't want a strict transactional system here. Data velocity and volume is high. NoSQL databases like DynamoDB also support transactions. But any scale-out DB on the cloud like DynamoDB or equivalent in Azure or GCP will work in this case. For database sharding — our data storage is around 5.5 terabytes. Usually we do sharding if the data storage is more than 50 terabytes. So based upon data storage alone we don't need sharding. However the write requests — clicks — coming to our system at peak is 100k, ad creation is at peak 1,000 per second. In total we have updates happening at around 100k per second at peak. This doesn't do well if we don't shard. Sharding is required generally when write requests are more than 10k, 15k or 20k per second. Hence in light of the number of write requests we're getting per second, we'll be sharding our database.
training_pairs.follow_up: Should ad creation and click processing be synchronous or asynchronous?

training_pairs.question: Should ad creation and click processing be synchronous or asynchronous?
training_pairs.answer: For the ad creation if you see we have a peak of 1,000 requests per second. Now based upon that you might not just use a Kafka — it's not required to have a pub-sub system sitting in — you can just process the request synchronously. So maybe Kafka sitting in between can be an overkill. But however I thought, once we receive a new ad there might be several validation steps that needs to be executed — if the ad is duplicate or not, trust and safety, compliance, etc — and hence it might take more things to be done and we can process the ad asynchronously. Customer experience will be better because we can just process it asynchronously and we can say hey we received your ad we are going to process it and latency that they see on their side will be significantly lower. But I mean there is no right and wrong answer in system design so you can still say I'll do synchronous processing given that number of requests is only 1,000 per second and as per requirements we are not given to handle the duplicate checks, trust and safety issues, etc. Now for clicks we have a peak of 100,000 per second. Async processing will be really good for processing the clicks because the meat of the problem is to let the user see the ads. Clicks we can process in an async way — it can decouple the processing of the clicks and we can also batch the clicks before we update the database. By consuming the clicks, batching them, and then making a one batch call to the database — that way we can reduce the load on the database. If our peak is even more than that, let's say 1 million per second, generally it would be better if we do an async processing.
training_pairs.follow_up: How would you approach batching the clicks?

training_pairs.question: How would you approach batching the clicks?
training_pairs.answer: So we know that our latency SLO for processing the clicks and updating the ad score is 3 minutes. We can leverage this SLO — you can batch the clicks for example based upon 30 seconds, or if the number of clicks we have got to process is 10,000 — all of this is configurable, maybe 15 seconds or 1 minute, whatever occurs faster. And then we can just aggregate the scores across the ads. For example in 10,000 clicks we have 5,000 clicks for one ad — we can just create — okay if the click yields plus five for that ad and if there are 5,000 clicks for ad one we'll have 25,000 as a score to be added for ad one. And again, this will reduce the load on the database and the batch API call — and batching will really help in this case.
training_pairs.follow_up: What caching strategy would you use?

training_pairs.question: What caching strategy would you use and which cache technology?
training_pairs.answer: So we have Redis or Memcached as our options. So I'll prefer Redis in this case because Redis has some good data structures like sorted sets which we want to use — we don't need to reinvent the wheel by implementing these in Memcached. It's a very plain or vanilla cache. So, sorted set is a combination of hash table and skip list. Hash table will have the entry which is the ad and the score for that ad and skip list will have the category — which means for example iPhone — and the sorted list of ads sorted by scores. And in terms of cache storage — let's assume we have 100,000 product types. We want to store top 25 per product type. And let's assume record size is 1 KB per ad entry. Which translates to 100k product types multiplied by 25 multiplied by 1 KB record size — which is 2.5 GB — which we can easily put in cache. Nowadays we have systems which can have 1 terabyte of cache easily. Yes, cache is expensive, but we'll have faster results because it will be an in-memory processing and we can provide a better customer experience and reduce latency to our customers.
training_pairs.follow_up: How would you handle ambiguous searches like 'gifts for a 10-year-old'?

training_pairs.question: How would you handle ambiguous searches like 'gifts for a 10-year-old' that don't map directly to a product type?
training_pairs.answer: So how to handle the case when user searches for example 'gifts for 10 years old'? The first approach is — we can add a tags field to the database. For example, if it's a comic book product type, we can add tags in our database like 'gifts for 10-year-old' or 'children's gift' etc. And when the ad entry is added to the database this lambda will process and add to the cache based upon the score. And there will be three sorted sets in Redis — for example for 'comic book', for 'gifts for 10 year old', for 'children's gift' — all the same entries. If someone searches 'gifts for 10 years old' I can just look into the cache, hey if there's an entry for this just get me that list and return it. Now this approach is good but it has several limits — right, for example you cannot predict what the user will be searching for. So you cannot predict what user can do in their query. So this approach is not very solid and also it is causing repeated data entries which can cause sync issues and redundant data is there and will lead to more memory consumption and duplication. So we have another approach — we can add a machine learning based context service. Whenever the user searches a keyword the context service will return some relevant product types. For example, if someone searches 'gifts for 10 years old', this query will be passed to context service — it will return some keywords for example 'stationary', 'comic book', or 'a toy', etc. And based upon that the search service will just search in the cache for those product types, get top 10 for, you know, three product types, and we'll sort the overall results in memory and we'll return the response to the user. So this way it's more extensible, it's better — you don't have to rely on what user is going to search — and no data replication or more memory consumption etc.
training_pairs.follow_up: How do you prevent new seller starvation?

training_pairs.question: How do you prevent new sellers from being starved — never shown because they have no click history?
training_pairs.answer: How to handle a case to ensure new seller ads are shown to the customer — let's say I'm a new seller onboarding and I'm submitting a new sponsored ad product for a product type which is quite popular and which people have already clicked. And if I'm a new entry into the market, Google will not show my product because my score has not been generated — I don't have any score — and for this product type the sellers who have more score for their products will eventually be shown. So it's more like a starvation problem. So how do I deal with such a case? For any new seller ad, provide a default score which is more than the current maximum score for the product type so that we give them a fair chance. And this will ensure that new sellers or new ads are promoted and not being starved in our system. And that is what we want to have because as a business we want different sellers to get products in our system so that we can have sponsored ads and if they click on that, if users click on that, we get — as Google as a business — we get referral fees.
training_pairs.follow_up: How do you scale click processing to handle millions of clicks per minute?

training_pairs.question: How do you scale click processing to handle millions of clicks per minute?
training_pairs.answer: So how do we make sure we process the clicks very fast also given that we are doing an IO call to process each and every click? The solution here is to have a load balancing design. Let's say how Kafka works — Kafka has several broker machines and let's say we have 10 partitions for click topic in Kafka and these partitions are P0 to P9, all 10 partitions. We can create multiple instances of our click processor service — let's call it click processor 1, 2, 3, 4 and 5. But think about like, each cluster has one primary and two secondaries. And if primary goes down then secondary becomes primary. You can have a ZooKeeper like system which is monitoring this. But overall the idea is that we load balance how many partitions are being consumed by a primary. So if we do 2:1 mapping — two Kafka partitions to one click processor instance mapping — so P0, P1 are being consumed by click processor one, P2, P3 by two, P4, P5 by click processor 3, P6, P7 by click processor 4 and P8, P9 by click processor 5. That way we are doing parallel processing. We are moving fast and within a partition the messages are in order. So if our partition key is based upon an ad product — this means that for all the clicks happening for that ad it is coming in in order and we are processing in order. We are not doing out of order processing within a partition. So that way you can scale your system and you can process the messages faster and enable parallel processing.
training_pairs.follow_up: How do you ensure correctness in processing the ad score — specifically preventing double-counting after a crash?

training_pairs.question: How do you ensure correctness in processing the ad score — specifically preventing double-counting after a crash?
training_pairs.answer: So the situation is: let's say the click processor updates the ad scores in the database after processing the batch but it didn't commit the offset in Kafka — or the messages which it has processed, it missed telling to Kafka that hey I processed these messages — and the primary click processor crashes. Now upon restart, click processor will again process the uncommitted messages, thinking that they're not being processed and the ad scores will be incorrectly updated. In this case the scores for the ads which have been processed will lead to doubling — not doubling the entire score, but doubling whatever number it had previously when it processed the batch — and it can cause accuracy, correctness issues, bias in our system. So how do we handle such a case? The solution here is to add a new field in the database called 'message offset' for every ad and before processing the click event per ad the click processor will check if the message offset of the ad click event is greater than the message offset for that ad in the database. If it is greater then sure we can process it because it's a new message. But if it is less than or equal to what the offset we have stored in the database it will ignore that click event because it's already been processed. For example we have messages at offset 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6 and ad ID 1 appears at offsets 1, 2, 4, 6. After initial processing, ad ID 1 score is 20 — four clicks times five points — and the maximum offset stored is 6. If the processor crashes and reprocesses, it will ignore all messages for ad ID 1 because offset 1 is less than 6, offset 2 is less than 6, offset 4 is less than 6, offset 6 is equal to 6. So using this technique we can make sure we have correctness in our system.
